annotations.label: Regular language (via regular expression)

anchor.snippet: is a formal language that can be defined by a regular expression

annotations.note: Regular expressions and their matched languages are defined, but Mathlib does not define language regularity via regex nor link it to the automaton-based `IsRegular`.

annotations.label: Regular language (via finite automaton)

anchor.snippet: a regular language can be defined as a language recognised by a finite automaton

annotations.note: `Language.IsRegular L` is defined exactly as existence of a finite-state DFA whose accepted language is `L`.

annotations.label: Kleene's theorem

anchor.snippet: The equivalence of regular expressions and finite automata is known as Kleene's theorem

annotations.note: The RegularExpressions file explicitly notes that equivalence of regular expressions and DFAs/NFAs is not yet shown in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Recursive definition of regular languages

anchor.snippet: is defined recursively as follows

annotations.note: The inductive `RegularExpression` (zero, epsilon, char, plus, comp, star) with `matches'` encodes this recursive construction, but it is not tied to a recursive `IsRegular` predicate.

annotations.label: Finite languages are regular

anchor.snippet: All finite languages are regular

annotations.note: No lemma stating that singleton/finite languages are regular exists, despite the `IsRegular` framework.

annotations.label: Even number of a's / a's then b's

anchor.snippet: Other typical examples include the language consisting of all strings

annotations.note: These specific example languages are not constructed or shown regular in Mathlib.

annotations.label: a^n b^n is not regular

anchor.snippet: A simple example of a language that is not regular

annotations.note: The pumping lemma (`DFA.pumping_lemma`, also `NFA.pumping_lemma`) — the standard tool to prove non-regularity — is formalized, but this specific non-regularity result is not.

annotations.label: Equivalent characterizations of regular languages

anchor.snippet: satisfies the following equivalent properties

annotations.note: DFA/NFA equivalence (`NFA.toDFA_correct`, `DFA.toNFA_correct`) and the Myhill–Nerode characterization are formalized, but other equivalences (regex, MSO logic, regular grammars) are not.

annotations.label: Closure under Boolean, regular, and trio operations

anchor.snippet: are closed under various operations

annotations.note: Closure under complement, union (`.add`), intersection (`.inf`) and reversal (`.reverse`) is proven, but closure under concatenation, Kleene star, and homomorphisms is not.

annotations.label: Closure under quotient with arbitrary language

anchor.snippet: closed under quotients with arbitrary languages

annotations.note: `Language.leftQuotient` (quotient by a word) exists and underlies Myhill–Nerode, but closure of regular languages under quotient by an arbitrary language is not proven.

annotations.label: Reverse automaton construction

anchor.snippet: can be obtained by reversing all transitions

annotations.note: `NFA.reverse` builds the automaton by reversing all transitions and swapping start/accept, with `NFA.mem_accepts_reverse` proving it accepts the reversed language.

annotations.label: Equivalence of two DFAs is decidable

anchor.snippet: it is decidable whether they accept the same language

annotations.note: No decidability result for equivalence of DFAs/regular languages is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Containment, disjointness, emptiness, universality, membership decidable

anchor.snippet: the following problems are also decidable

annotations.note: Membership is decidable via `RegularExpression.rmatch` (with a `DecidablePred (· ∈ P.matches')` instance), but emptiness, universality, containment and disjointness decidability are not formalized.

annotations.label: Universality NP-complete for singleton alphabet

anchor.snippet: the universality problem is NP-complete already for a singleton alphabet

annotations.note: No complexity-theoretic results about the universality problem are formalized.

annotations.label: Universality PSPACE-complete for larger alphabets

anchor.snippet: that problem is PSPACE-complete

annotations.note: PSPACE-completeness of the universality problem is not formalized.

annotations.label: Universality with squaring has exponential space lower bound

anchor.snippet: the universality problem has an exponential space lower bound

annotations.note: Exponential space lower bounds for regular-expression universality are not formalized.

annotations.label: Regular languages as minimal elementary substructure

anchor.snippet: its minimal elementary substructure consists precisely of regular languages

annotations.note: This model-theoretic characterization of regular languages is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: REGULAR equals DSPACE(O(1))

anchor.snippet: the decision problems that can be solved in constant space

annotations.note: No space-complexity class characterization of REGULAR is formalized.

annotations.label: REGULAR ≠ AC^0

anchor.snippet: contains the parity problem of determining whether the number of 1 bits

annotations.note: Circuit-complexity separations involving AC^0 and parity are not formalized in this context.

annotations.label: REGULAR does not contain AC^0

anchor.snippet: REGULAR does not contain AC

annotations.note: The non-containment of AC^0 in REGULAR is not formalized.

annotations.label: Space lower bound for non-regular languages

anchor.snippet: it requires a machine with at least

annotations.note: Space lower bounds for recognizing non-regular languages are not formalized.

annotations.label: DSPACE(o(log log n)) equals regular languages

anchor.snippet: equals the class of regular languages

annotations.note: The DSPACE(o(log log n)) = REGULAR collapse result is not formalized.

annotations.label: Every regular language is context-free

anchor.snippet: every regular language is context-free

annotations.note: Both `Language.IsRegular` and `Language.IsContextFree` are defined, but the inclusion of regular languages into context-free languages is not proven.

annotations.label: Equal number of a's and b's is context-free but not regular

anchor.snippet: is context-free but not regular

annotations.note: This specific separating example between context-free and regular languages is not formalized.

annotations.label: Finite languages subclass

anchor.snippet: Finite languages, those containing only a finite number of words

annotations.note: There is no defined subclass of finite languages within the formal-language hierarchy in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Star-free languages

anchor.snippet: Star-free languages , those that can be described by a regular expression constructed from the empty symbol

annotations.note: Star-free languages are not defined in Mathlib (no `StarFree` notion exists).

annotations.label: Ordinary generating function of a language

anchor.section: Number of words in a regular language
anchor.snippet: The ordinary generating function for L is the formal power series

annotations.note: The generating function counting words of each length in a language is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generating function of a regular language is rational

anchor.section: Number of words in a regular language
anchor.snippet: is a rational function if L is regular

annotations.note: Rationality of the generating function of a regular language is not formalized.

annotations.label: Word-count sequence is constant-recursive

anchor.section: Number of words in a regular language
anchor.snippet: is constant-recursive

annotations.note: No result that the word-count sequence of a regular language satisfies a linear (constant-recursive) recurrence is present.

annotations.label: Dyck language non-regularity via Catalan numbers

anchor.section: Number of words in a regular language
anchor.snippet: witnessing the non-regularity of the Dyck language

annotations.note: Catalan numbers and `DyckWord` exist in Mathlib, but the non-regularity of the Dyck language is not formalized.

annotations.label: Asymptotic word count for regular languages

anchor.section: Number of words in a regular language
anchor.snippet: In general, for every regular language there exists a constant

annotations.note: Asymptotic growth bounds on the number of words in a regular language are not formalized.

annotations.label: Zeta function of a language

anchor.section: Number of words in a regular language
anchor.snippet: The zeta function of a language L is

annotations.note: The zeta function of a formal language is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Zeta function of regular language not generally rational

anchor.section: Number of words in a regular language
anchor.snippet: is not in general rational, but that of an arbitrary cyclic language is

annotations.note: Results about rationality of zeta functions of regular or cyclic languages are not formalized.
